name: Olorun
description: Olorun, also known as Olodumare, stands as the supreme being in the Yoruba religion of West Africa, specifically Nigeria and Benin. Olorun is considered the ultimate source of all existence, possessing immense power and wisdom. While often described as a singular, all-powerful entity, Olorun is also understood as embodying the concept of the divine in its totality. Olorun is not typically directly worshipped through shrines or sacrifices, as the deity is seen as being beyond the immediate reach of humans. Instead, worship is directed toward the Orishas, the divine emissaries who act as intermediaries between Olorun and humanity. Olorun's influence permeates all aspects of Yoruba life, from the natural world to social structures, and is often invoked in prayers and ceremonies. The concept of ase, the divine life force, is intrinsically linked to Olorun, as the deity is the source of this vital energy that animates all things. Olorun's nature is abstract and profound, representing the ultimate mystery of creation and the divine order.
subtitle: The Supreme Being

keyMyths.title: The Creation of the World
keyMyths.description: Olorun tasked Obatala with creating the earth, but Obatala became intoxicated with palm wine and failed in his duty. Oduduwa then took over, completing the task by scattering earth upon the waters from a snail shell, and releasing a hen to spread the earth, thus creating land. This myth illustrates the importance of responsibility and the consequences of failure in the face of divine mandates, while also highlighting the collaborative nature of creation.
keyMyths.source: Idowu, E. Bolaji. *Olódùmarè: God in Yoruba Belief*. Longman, 1962.

keyMyths.title: The Giving of Ase
keyMyths.description: Olorun bestowed Ase, the divine power to make things happen, upon the Orishas and humanity. This gift allowed them to influence the world and fulfill their destinies. The amount of Ase granted varied among the Orishas, determining their individual powers and responsibilities. Understanding and harnessing Ase is central to Yoruba spirituality, as it empowers individuals to manifest their desires and navigate the complexities of life, connecting them directly to the divine.
keyMyths.source: Awolalu, J. Omosade. *Yoruba Beliefs and Sacrificial Rites*. Longman, 1979.

keyMyths.title: Olorun and the Orishas
keyMyths.description: While Olorun is the supreme being, the Orishas are the intermediaries who interact with the human world. Each Orisha embodies specific aspects of nature and human experience, such as love, justice, and healing. They act as messengers and representatives of Olorun, carrying out the divine will and providing guidance to humanity. This hierarchical structure allows for a more accessible and personalized form of worship, while still acknowledging the ultimate authority of Olorun.
keyMyths.source: Abimbola, Wande. *Ifá: An Exposition of Ifá Literary Corpus*. Ibadan University Press, 1976.

sources.author: Idowu, E. Bolaji
